rationale: Both titles cover the same rare dual-complication case after LAAC. Title X is clean and readable but generic — 'elderly patient' is vague and it conveys no finding beyond the mere occurrence of the events. Title Y adds three clinically meaningful details absent from X: patient age (86 years), sex (woman), and the central management challenge ('dynamically individualized antithrombotic management'), which is the actual clinical contribution of a case report. For PACE, which frequently publishes case reports with procedural and peri-procedural complications, the management dimension is the publishable lesson, not just the adverse events. Y communicates why the case matters, making it substantially more informative and appealing to reviewers and readers. The slightly longer construction is justified by the added precision.
loser_weakness: Title X omits the clinical takeaway (individualized antithrombotic management) and replaces specific patient descriptors with the vague phrase 'elderly patient,' leaving readers unable to gauge the case's novelty or significance from the title alone.
